worth_it_explanation: A Google Ad Manager (GAM) report is only as trustworthy as the way it is scoped, so a query with the wrong date range, time zone, or metric choice produces a confident-looking number that points the publisher in the wrong direction. The check is fast because it reads the reports the publisher already relies on rather than building new ones. Catching a time-zone mismatch or a misread metric here prevents a yield or pacing decision made on a number that does not mean what the publisher thinks it means.
intro: Reporting in GAM drives the publisher's view of revenue, demand, and inventory performance, and every number depends on how the query that produced it was scoped. A report's date range, time zone, dimension breakdown, and filter set each change the figure, and the metrics GAM exposes are easy to confuse with one another. These checks confirm that the reports the publisher trusts are scoped consistently and that the metrics driving decisions are read as GAM defines them.

audit_checks.title: Report queries are scoped in ways that mislead
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A report scoped with a mismatched time zone, an unstated filter, or an unexpected date range returns a number that looks authoritative but does not represent what the reader assumes. Decisions about yield, pacing, and demand get made on that number, so the error propagates into actions rather than staying contained in a spreadsheet.

educate.base:
A report in Google Ad Manager (GAM) is the product of four scoping choices, and each one changes the number it returns. The date range sets the window. The time zone decides where each day starts and ends. The dimensions decide how the totals are broken down. The filters decide which rows are counted at all. A figure quoted without those four choices stated is ambiguous. The same query run two different ways produces two different numbers that are both correct for their own scope.

Time zone is the scoping choice most likely to mislead. GAM offers a network time zone and an Ad Exchange time zone, and the network time zone is the default. When a publisher compares a GAM figure scoped to the network time zone against a partner's figure scoped to a different time zone, the day boundaries do not line up, and the two totals look like a discrepancy when they are only counting different hours. The same mismatch appears when one report uses one time zone and a later report on the same inventory uses another.

Filters are the second source of confusion. A report filtered to a single ad unit, a single country, or a single demand channel reports that slice, not the network total. When the filter is not stated, a reader treats a filtered figure as a total and concludes the network is underperforming when the report only ever covered part of it. The date range carries the same risk on a smaller scale, for example a report that runs through yesterday compared against one that includes today's partial data.

A few signs point to reports that are scoped in misleading ways:
  • The same metric shows different values across two reports the publisher treats as equivalent
  • A GAM total and a partner total are compared without confirming they share a time zone
  • A figure is quoted as a network total when the report carries a filter that narrows it
  • A report mixes a partial current day with full prior days in one trend line
You may want to settle on one time zone for the reports the publisher relies on and label what each report counts, so a figure carries its scope with it rather than depending on memory.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media property with several monetization partners, the day-boundary mismatch between GAM's network time zone and each partner's reporting time zone is the most frequent reason a GAM revenue figure does not tie to a partner's. You may want to standardize on one time zone across the reports you reconcile and note it on each export.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content property where ad revenue sits alongside merchandising data, you may want to confirm the ad reports and the commerce reports share a date range and time zone before comparing them, so a difference in scope is not read as a difference in performance.

learn_more.source_title: Best practices and tips for reporting

impactful_updates.update_title: Classic Reports deactivated in favor of Interactive reports
impactful_updates.update_summary: Google Ad Manager completed the migration of reporting to Interactive reports, with the classic 'Reports' tool fully deactivated in June 2026. The scoping choices that drive a report's accuracy, such as date range, time zone, dimensions, and filters, carry over to Interactive reports, so any saved queries the publisher relies on should be confirmed in the new reporting experience.

investigate.intro: Review the date range, time zone, dimensions, and filters on the reports the publisher relies on, and confirm that any reports compared against each other or against a partner share the same scope.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Reporting', then 'Reports', and open one of the reports the publisher uses to judge revenue or performance.
Note the report's date range, then check the time-zone setting and record whether it is the network time zone or the Ad Exchange time zone.
Read the dimensions and filters applied, and note whether the report is scoped to the full network or narrowed to an ad unit, a country, a demand channel, or another slice.
Open a second report the publisher treats as equivalent and compare its date range, time zone, dimensions, and filters against the first. Record any place where the two reports differ in scope.
Where a GAM figure is reconciled against a partner's number, confirm the two cover the same time zone and the same date window.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each report the publisher relies on, its date range, time zone, dimension breakdown, and filter set, and notes whether reports that are compared against each other or against a partner share the same scope. A set of reports that use a consistent time zone, state their filters, and are compared only against equally scoped figures passes. A report compared against a differently scoped report or partner figure, or a filtered figure read as a network total, is a fail recorded with the report name and the scoping difference at fault.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the date range, time zone, dimensions, and filters for each report above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Google Ad Manager (GAM) reports {{site_name}} relies on are scoped in ways that can mislead. A report's date range, time zone, dimensions, and filters each change the number it returns, and reports compared against each other or against a partner did not consistently share the same scope, so a figure read as a network total in one place counts something narrower in another. {{voice_recommend}} settling on one time zone across the reports used for reconciliation, labeling what each report counts, and confirming that any two figures compared together share the same date range, time zone, and filters. After the change, rerun the key reports and confirm the totals tie out where they are expected to.

audit_checks.title: Key metrics are misinterpreted
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Google Ad Manager exposes metrics that count different things but sound interchangeable, so reading one as another, for example treating filtered requests as total requests or measured impressions as viewable, produces a wrong conclusion about demand or performance. The decisions that follow inherit the misreading.

educate.base:
Google Ad Manager (GAM) reports expose many metrics, and several pairs are easy to confuse because the names sound close while the definitions differ. Reading one metric as if it were another is not a configuration error in the account. It is a reading error, and it leads to a conclusion about demand, fill, or performance that the data does not support.

Ad requests are one place this happens. Total ad requests counts every request, including the ones that returned no ad, while a filtered or eligibility-narrowed request count covers only a subset. A publisher who reads a narrowed count as the total understates how much inventory was actually called, and a fill or match rate calculated against the wrong denominator comes out wrong in turn.

Coverage and fill rate are a second pair. Both describe how much inventory was filled, but they are calculated against different bases and answer different questions, so quoting one while meaning the other misstates how the inventory is performing. The third pair is measured against viewable impressions. A measured impression is one that Active View was able to assess, while a viewable impression is one that met the Media Rating Council (MRC) viewability standard, namely 50 percent of the ad's pixels in view for at least one continuous second for display. Treating measured as viewable overstates how much of the served inventory was actually seen.

Each of these reads has a definition behind it that GAM publishes. You may want to confirm what a metric counts in the reporting metric reference before acting on it, especially when a number is about to drive a pricing, demand, or inventory decision. A metric understood as defined supports the decision. A metric read as its near-neighbor undermines it while still looking precise.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media property judging programmatic demand and yield, the request and fill metrics are the ones most likely to be misread. Total ad requests, filtered requests, coverage, and fill rate all describe the same inventory from different angles. You may want to confirm which metric each yield report is built on before reading it as a demand signal.

learn_more.source_title: Overview of Viewability and Active View

investigate.intro: Review how the publisher reads its core metrics, and confirm each definition against the GAM reporting documentation before any of them is used to draw a conclusion.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Reporting', then 'Reports', and list the metrics the publisher uses to judge demand, fill, and inventory performance.
For each request-related metric, note whether the report uses total ad requests or a filtered or eligibility-narrowed count, and confirm which one any fill or match rate is calculated against.
For coverage and fill rate, confirm which metric the publisher quotes and that it matches the question being asked of it.
For impression quality, note whether the publisher reads measured impressions or viewable impressions, and confirm the Active View metric used reports viewability rather than measurability.
Check each definition against the reporting metric reference in GAM Help, and record any metric the publisher reads as its near-neighbor rather than as defined.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each core metric the publisher acts on, how the publisher reads it and the GAM definition it should carry. A set of metrics read as GAM defines them, with request counts, coverage, fill rate, and viewability each used for their own question, passes. A metric read as a near-neighbor, for example a filtered request count treated as total ad requests or a measured impression treated as viewable, is a fail recorded with the metric name and the misreading at fault.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record how the publisher reads each core metric and the GAM definition it should carry above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that some of the core Google Ad Manager (GAM) metrics {{site_name}} acts on are read as near-neighbors rather than as GAM defines them. Metrics such as total ad requests versus filtered requests, coverage versus fill rate, and measured versus viewable impressions count different things, so reading one as another produces a conclusion about demand or performance that the data does not support. {{voice_recommend}} confirming each metric against the GAM reporting metric reference, aligning request counts, fill calculations, and viewability figures with their published definitions, and noting the definition alongside each metric in the reports that drive decisions. After the change, reread the key reports with the corrected definitions and confirm the conclusions still hold.
